flashcards.front: What is the formal definition of Real Numbers (ℝ)?
flashcards.back: ℝ is the union of rational numbers (ℚ) and irrational numbers, forming the complete set of all numbers that can be positioned on a number line.

flashcards.front: State the complete number system hierarchy.
flashcards.back: ℕ ⊂ ℤ ⊂ ℚ ⊂ ℝ, where: ℕ = Natural, ℤ = Integers, ℚ = Rationals, ℝ = Reals.

flashcards.front: What does it mean that real numbers are 'dense'?
flashcards.back: Between any two distinct real numbers, there exist infinitely many other real numbers. No gaps exist on the real number line.

flashcards.front: What does it mean that real numbers are 'complete'?
flashcards.back: The Completeness Axiom: every non-empty set of real numbers with an upper bound has a least upper bound. This ensures the number line has no missing points.

flashcards.front: Why can we measure any physical quantity with real numbers?
flashcards.back: Real numbers are ordered and dense, filling every point on a continuous line. Whether measuring distance, time, or temperature, any value can be represented by a real number.

flashcards.front: Are all real numbers either rational or irrational?
flashcards.back: Yes. By definition, ℝ = ℚ ∪ {irrationals}. Every real number must be one or the other—either expressible as p/q, or possessing a non-repeating, non-terminating decimal.

flashcards.front: Give an example of a rational real number and an irrational real number.
flashcards.back: Rational: 3/4 or 0.75 (repeating decimal). Irrational: √2 ≈ 1.414... or π ≈ 3.14159... (non-repeating, non-terminating).

flashcards.front: Explain why real numbers are closed under multiplication and division.
flashcards.back: Multiplying two reals always yields a real. Dividing one real by a non-zero real always yields a real. No operation escapes the set ℝ. (This differs from earlier sets: rationals are not closed under square roots, and integers are not closed under division.)
